vulnerabilities.title: Public _addCredIdPerAddress/_removeCredIdPerAddress Allow Permanent DoS on Share Sales
vulnerabilities.summary: _addCredIdPerAddress and _removeCredIdPerAddress are declared public despite underscore naming convention, allowing any external caller to corrupt per-address cred tracking data structures. An attacker can remove a victim's credId from the tracking array, causing subsequent sell operations to revert with WrongCredId, permanently locking the victim's shares and the ETH they represent on the bonding curve.

description.desc: Both functions declared 'public' with no access control modifier; any external caller can invoke them

vulnerabilities.impact: CRITICAL. Any user's shares can be permanently locked by any external caller at zero cost. The locked shares represent real ETH value on the bonding curve that becomes irretrievable. The only recovery path is a UUPS upgrade by the contract owner to fix the function visibility and manually reconstruct corrupted state. Additionally, _addCredIdPerAddress being public allows adding duplicate/fake entries that further corrupt tracking data and could cause additional issues in getPositionsForCurator and other view functions.

vulnerabilities.title: Unsigned Quantity in signatureClaim/merkleClaim Allows Arbitrary NFT Minting
vulnerabilities.summary: The mintArgs_ parameter (containing quantity and imageURI) is not included in the signed encodeData_ for signatureClaim or in the Merkle leaf for merkleClaim. An attacker with a valid signature or Merkle proof intended for quantity=1 can modify the quantity to mint up to maxSupply NFTs in a single transaction.

description.desc: Signature verification only covers encodeData_ which excludes mintArgs_ (quantity, imageURI); quantity is user-supplied without cryptographic binding

vulnerabilities.impact: CRITICAL. An attacker can exhaust the entire supply of any art by replaying a single valid signature with an inflated quantity. This permanently prevents legitimate users from minting, destroys the scarcity guarantee for art creators, and allows the attacker to accumulate all credential NFTs for a given art. The imageURI can also be set arbitrarily, corrupting the metadata of minted tokens.

vulnerabilities.title: Unbounded Loop in CuratorRewardsDistributor.distribute() Causes Permanent Fund Lock
vulnerabilities.summary: distribute() fetches ALL curator addresses via getCuratorAddresses(credId, 0, 0) and iterates through them twice (once for share counting, once for reward calculation). For popular creds with many curators, this exceeds the block gas limit, permanently locking accumulated rewards.

description.desc: Two loops over distributeAddresses (unbounded array from getCuratorAddresses(credId,0,0)) with external call getShareNumber per iteration

vulnerabilities.impact: HIGH severity. All accumulated curator rewards for any credId with sufficiently many (historical) curators become permanently irrecoverable. The CuratorRewardsDistributor is not upgradeable, and distribute() is the only function that can reduce balanceOf[credId]. There is no emergency withdrawal mechanism. The owner can only update phiRewardsContract and withdrawRoyalty - neither provides access to locked funds.

vulnerabilities.title: credMerkleRoot Overwrite When Creating New Art for Same CredId Invalidates Existing Claims
vulnerabilities.summary: credMerkleRoot[credChainId][credId] is a single shared slot per (chainId, credId) pair. When _initializePhiArt is called for a new art on the same credId, it overwrites the existing merkle root. This invalidates all pending Merkle claims for previously created arts that relied on the old root.

description.desc: credMerkleRoot[credChainId][credId] = merkleRootHash unconditionally overwrites on each art creation

vulnerabilities.impact: Legitimate users who were eligible for Art A under merkle root R1 lose their ability to claim. This is not recoverable by the users themselves. The protocol owner would need to deploy a new root or take corrective action. Additionally, this creates a potential griefing vector: anyone who can obtain a phiSigner signature for art creation on the same credId can invalidate all pending claims for that credId's arts.

vulnerabilities.title: Share Manipulation Before distribute() Extracts Disproportionate Curator Rewards
vulnerabilities.summary: An attacker can buy large quantities of shares for a credId just before calling distribute(), receiving a disproportionate share of accumulated rewards. After the 10-minute lock period, they sell their shares. If the reward exceeds the bonding curve spread and fees, the attack is profitable.

description.desc: Reward distribution proportional to share count at call time; no time-weighted average or snapshot mechanism
Affected function: buyShareCred

vulnerabilities.impact:
HIGH impact. Long-term curators who hold shares and contribute to cred curation have their rewards diluted by just-in-time buyers. The lack of time-weighting means a buyer who holds for 1 second receives the same reward per share as someone who held for months. This undermines the entire curator incentive mechanism.

Specific impacts:
- Legitimate curators receive diluted rewards proportional to how much the attacker dominates total supply
- Repeated exploitation drains curator reward pools systematically
- Economic incentive to curate long-term is diminished
- No recovery possible - distributed rewards cannot be clawed back

vulnerabilities.title: BondingCurve._getCreatorFee Missing Return Statement Causes Incorrect Price Quotes
vulnerabilities.summary: _getCreatorFee sets creatorFee=0 when supply_==0 but does not return, falling through to overwrite creatorFee with a non-zero royalty-based value. This causes getBuyPriceAfterFee and getSellPriceAfterFee to return inflated prices at supply=0, while getPriceData (used in actual trades) correctly returns 0.

description.desc: if (supply_ == 0) { creatorFee = 0; } — no return; falls through to creatorFee = (price_ * royaltyRate) / RATIO_BASE

vulnerabilities.impact: Medium severity. The defect causes view functions to return incorrect price quotes at supply=0. This creates a discrepancy between displayed prices and actual trade costs. Users relying on getBuyPriceAfterFee for maxPrice calculations would set unnecessarily high limits. Users or frontends comparing prices might be confused or deterred. No direct fund loss occurs since the actual trade uses getPriceData with the correct logic, but the code is objectively wrong — the _getCreatorFee function does not implement its intended behavior at supply=0.

vulnerabilities.title: BondingCurve.setCredContract Not Called in Deployment Script Causes All Trades to Fail
vulnerabilities.summary: In Deploy.s.sol, bondingCurve.setCredContract(address(credProxy)) is commented out. Without this call, BondingCurve.credContract remains address(0), causing all calls to getPriceData to revert when accessing credContract.getCreatorRoyalty() and credContract.protocolFeePercent().

description.desc: credContract.protocolFeePercent() and credContract.getCreatorRoyalty() called on potentially unset address

vulnerabilities.impact: Complete denial of service for all share trading functionality (buyShareCred, sellShareCred, batchBuyShareCred, batchSellShareCred) and cred creation (createCred). The protocol's core functionality is entirely broken until the owner calls setCredContract. Recovery is possible by having the owner call setCredContract(credProxyAddress). Additionally, all price query view functions (getBuyPriceAfterFee, getSellPriceAfterFee) also revert, meaning frontends cannot even display prices.

vulnerabilities.title: Unbounded protocolFeePercent can make sells revert (funds effectively trapped) due to fee underflow on sell payout
vulnerabilities.summary: Cred.setProtocolFeePercent() has no upper bound. If protocolFeePercent is set high enough such that protocolFee + creatorFee >= price on sells, then `_handleTrade` and batch sell paths compute `price - protocolFee - creatorFee` and will revert in Solidity 0.8+, blocking sells for affected creds/curators. This can lock curator positions economically (cannot exit) and potentially freeze trading liquidity.

description.desc: No cap on protocolFeePercent; sell payout implicitly uses `price - protocolFees[i] - creatorFees[i]` (batch totalPayout uses same subtraction), which will revert on underflow

vulnerabilities.impact: HIGH severity. The missing bounds check is a clear code defect. If exploited (by malicious owner or compromised key), all sell operations across all creds become impossible, trapping user capital in bonding curve positions with no alternative exit path. The defect is confirmed regardless of exploitation feasibility because the code lacks input validation that was clearly intended (as evidenced by PhiFactory having the check).

vulnerabilities.title: buyShareCredFor Enables Continuous Sell-Lock Griefing on Any Curator
vulnerabilities.summary: buyShareCredFor allows anyone to buy shares credited to any curator address. This sets lastTradeTimestamp[credId_][curator_] = block.timestamp, resetting the 10-minute sell lock period for the target curator. An attacker can repeatedly call buyShareCredFor with amount=1 for a victim's address, continuously preventing them from selling their shares. The cost is only the price of 1 share at current supply.

description.desc: lastTradeTimestamp[credId_][curator_] = block.timestamp — sets lock period for curator_ not for msg.sender. buyShareCredFor passes user-supplied curator_ address

vulnerabilities.impact: HIGH severity. Any curator can be indefinitely prevented from selling their shares. This is particularly damaging during price declines (e.g., when many users are selling, driving supply and price down), as the victim is forced to hold a depreciating position. The attack can also be used for competitive suppression, extortion, or to prevent a creator from selling. Using batchBuyShareCred, an attacker can lock a victim across multiple creds simultaneously in a single transaction.

vulnerabilities.title: Excess ETH Permanently Trapped in PhiFactory via claim() Self-Call Pattern
vulnerabilities.summary: PhiFactory.claim() calculates the exact mintFee and forwards only that amount via this.merkleClaim{value: mintFee} or this.signatureClaim{value: mintFee}. Any excess ETH sent by the user above mintFee remains in the PhiFactory contract with no refund to the caller. Additionally, within _processClaim, the refund logic sends to _msgSender() which resolves to address(this) when called through the self-call path, further trapping any overpayment.

description.desc: claim() calls this.merkleClaim{value: mintFee}(...) — only mintFee forwarded, excess stays in contract. In _processClaim: _msgSender().safeTransferETH(etherValue_ - mintFee) sends refund to address(this)

vulnerabilities.impact: Every user calling claim() or batchClaim() who sends more ETH than the exact mintFee loses the excess permanently (from their perspective). The excess accumulates in the PhiFactory and is extractable by the owner. This is a high-severity defect because: (1) users commonly send a buffer above estimated fees, (2) fee calculations may change between estimation and execution, and (3) there is no way for users to recover excess funds. The defect is particularly concerning because direct calls to signatureClaim() and merkleClaim() DO have refund logic (though it's broken in the self-call path), creating an inconsistency that makes the missing refund in claim() clearly unintentional.

vulnerabilities.title: withdrawFor Allows Forced Withdrawal of Any User's PhiRewards Balance Without Authorization
vulnerabilities.summary: RewardControl.withdrawFor(from, amount) has no access control—any caller can trigger a withdrawal from any address's balance, sending ETH to the 'from' address. With amount=0 (FULL_BALANCE sentinel), the entire balance is force-withdrawn. While funds go to the rightful owner, this enables griefing (forcing ETH onto contracts with expensive/reverting receive functions) and disrupts composability with protocols that accumulate PhiRewards balances.

description.desc: function withdrawFor(address from, uint256 amount) external { _withdraw(from, from, amount); } — no msg.sender check, no authorization requirement

vulnerabilities.impact: MEDIUM severity. While funds are not stolen (they go to the rightful owner), the lack of authorization is a clear code defect that: (1) Violates the principle that only account owners should control their funds' withdrawal timing, (2) Enables griefing attacks on contract accounts, (3) Breaks composability assumptions for protocols building on PhiRewards, (4) Creates inconsistency with the authorized withdraw() and withdrawWithSig() functions in the same contract.

vulnerabilities.title: handleRewardsAndGetValueSent Lacks Access Control Enabling Fake Reward Event Pollution
vulnerabilities.summary: PhiRewards.handleRewardsAndGetValueSent is external payable with NO access control. Anyone can call it directly (not just PhiNFT1155 contracts) by providing the required ETH. This deposits real rewards to arbitrary addresses and emits RewardsDeposit events that are indistinguishable from legitimate mint rewards, polluting the event log used for off-chain indexing and potentially gaming curator reward distributions via the curatorRewardsDistributor.deposit() call.

description.desc: handleRewardsAndGetValueSent is external payable with no modifier restricting caller. Any address can call with arbitrary artId_, credId_, addressesData_

vulnerabilities.impact: MEDIUM severity. The missing access control is a clear code defect. Impacts include: (1) Event log pollution making RewardsDeposit events unreliable for off-chain indexing, (2) Targeted inflation of curator reward pools for specific credIds at a cost of curateReward per fake mint (0.00005 ETH), (3) Potential to game curator reward distribution when attacker holds dominant share position in target credId. While the attacker must spend real ETH and cannot directly profit from pure cycling (they lose the curate reward portion), the ability to artificially inflate specific credId reward pools and pollute event logs represents a real integrity violation.

vulnerabilities.title: Cross-Chain Signature Replay Allows Unauthorized Minting of Arbitrary Arts
vulnerabilities.summary: The `signatureClaim` function decodes `chainId` from the user-provided payload but fails to validate it against `block.chainid`. This allows signatures from one chain to be replayed on another chain.

description.desc: abi.decode skips the 6th parameter (chainId) and does not verify it against block.chainid

vulnerabilities.impact: This is a genuine code defect. The chainId field is included in the signed payload by design (the claim() function encodes block.chainid), proving the developers intended chain-specific signatures. However, signatureClaim never enforces this binding. Impact includes: (1) unauthorized minting of arts on chains where the user had no valid claim, (2) violation of per-chain mint tracking since credMinted uses the art's credChainId (from storage) not the signature's chainId, (3) potential minting of arts the signer never authorized for that user on the target chain.

vulnerabilities.title: Excess ETH in createArt is Refunded to Factory and Trapped
vulnerabilities.summary: When a user calls `createArt` with excess ETH, the factory forwards the ETH to the NFT contract. The NFT contract refunds the excess to `_msgSender()` (the Factory). The Factory has no logic to forward this refund back to the user, permanently trapping the ETH.

description.desc: _msgSender().safeTransferETH(msg.value - artFee) sends refund to the Factory, not the user
Affected function: _useExistingNFTContract

vulnerabilities.impact: Currently: Denial of Service / Bad UX — users sending any excess ETH in createArt get their entire transaction reverted. Latent: If PhiFactory is upgraded to include receive(), excess ETH would be permanently trapped and extractable only by the owner via withdraw(). The code defect (wrong refund address) exists independent of whether the Factory can receive ETH.

vulnerabilities.title: Advanced Token URIs Are Lost on Secondary Market Transfer
vulnerabilities.summary: The `advancedTokenURI` is keyed by the user's address during minting. When the NFT is transferred via `safeTransferFrom`, this mapping is not updated for the new owner. The new owner will see the default URI instead of the advanced metadata.

description.desc: uri() checks advancedTokenURI[tokenId_][minter_] where minter_ is the current owner, but state is only set for the original minter

vulnerabilities.impact: For transferable (non-soulbound) tokens with advanced URIs, metadata that users potentially paid for (through mint fees) is lost to new owners upon transfer. This breaks secondary market expectations, as the unique visual/metadata properties that give the NFT premium value disappear immediately upon receipt by a buyer. The standard ERC1155 uri(uint256) function always returns the default URI regardless, so even the original minter's advanced URI is only visible through the non-standard uri(uint256, address) function.

vulnerabilities.title: Unbounded Royalty BPS Allows Artist to Break ERC2981 Integrations
vulnerabilities.summary: The `_updateRoyalties` function does not enforce a maximum limit on `royaltyBPS`. An artist can set the royalty to > 10000 (100%), causing the calculated royalty amount to exceed the sale price.

description.desc: Missing require(configuration.royaltyBPS <= 10000)

vulnerabilities.impact: MEDIUM severity. The artist (who must be the art creator) can unilaterally break secondary market trading for all holders of that art's NFTs. While no direct financial extraction occurs, holders lose liquidity and the ability to transfer/sell their tokens on ERC2981-compliant marketplaces. The attack cost is minimal (one transaction's gas), and the effect is immediate and persistent. Note that the Cred share royalties in Cred.sol DO have a MAX_ROYALTY_RANGE=5000 check, making this omission in CreatorRoyaltiesControl inconsistent with the project's own patterns.

vulnerabilities.title: Artist Can Retroactively Soulbind NFTs to Prevent Secondary Market Sales
vulnerabilities.summary: The `soulBounded` status is checked dynamically during `safeTransferFrom`. The artist can change this status at any time via `updateArtSettings`, retroactively locking previously minted and transferable NFTs.

description.desc: soulBounded(ids_[i]) is queried dynamically during transfer rather than being immutable per token

vulnerabilities.impact: HIGH - All holders of the affected art's tokens lose transferability instantly. NFTs listed on secondary markets become unliquidatable. The artist can use this as leverage to extort holders, manipulate market perception, or grief the community. The bidirectional toggle (can be set back to false) adds an additional manipulation vector. This violates user expectations established at mint time and constitutes a rug-pull capability.

vulnerabilities.title: PhiFactory missing fee parameter validation allows artist to set mintFee arbitrarily high, causing user fund traps/UX failure during claim flows
vulnerabilities.summary: updateArtSettings permits art creators to set mintFee_ without any upper bound. Since required payment is computed as computeMintReward(quantity, arts[artId].mintFee) + quantity*mintProtocolFee, extreme mintFee can make claims economically impossible or lead to frequent user overpayment attempts that interact badly with claim() self-call/refund behavior (excess ETH becomes stuck). This can be weaponized as an operational DoS on minting for a given art, especially when art is advertised as availa

description.desc: art.mintFee = mintFee_; // no upper bound or sanity check

vulnerabilities.impact: MEDIUM severity. The defect is a missing input validation that enables art-specific DoS. Impact is limited to individual arts (not system-wide), requires the art creator's action, and is reversible. However: (1) it's inconsistent with other fee parameter validations in the codebase, (2) arithmetic overflow can cause confusing reverts for users, (3) in a permissionless art creation ecosystem, malicious creators are a realistic threat.

vulnerabilities.title: Pagination Bug in `getPositionsForCurator` Causes Reverts and Data Corruption
vulnerabilities.summary: The `getPositionsForCurator` function uses the loop variable `i` (which starts at `start_`) to index into the newly allocated `credIds` and `amounts` arrays (which are sized `stopIndex - start_`). If `start_ > 0`, `i` will exceed the bounds of the arrays, causing a revert.

description.desc: credIds[i] = credId; amounts[i] = amount; where i can be >= credIds.length

vulnerabilities.impact: Medium severity. This is a view function bug that breaks pagination functionality. While it does not directly cause financial loss, it breaks core protocol functionality for reading user positions. Frontends and integrating contracts relying on pagination will fail. Users with many positions cannot retrieve their full portfolio data through this function. The bug requires a contract upgrade to fix.

vulnerabilities.title: PhiFactory.claim/batchClaim self-call flow can trap user ETH (excess value not refunded to original caller)
vulnerabilities.summary: PhiFactory.claim computes `mintFee` and then calls `this.merkleClaim{value: mintFee}` / `this.signatureClaim{value: mintFee}`. Any ETH sent to claim above mintFee remains in PhiFactory. Additionally, inside _processClaim, refunds are sent to `_msgSender()` (which becomes `address(this)` due to external self-call), so any refund from overpayment in the inner call would not reach the user. batchClaim forwards per-item values similarly via `this.claim`, potentially compounding stuck ETH.

description.desc: external self-call changes msg.sender to address(this), causing refunds (if any) to be routed to factory, and excess msg.value at outer level never forwarded/refunded

vulnerabilities.impact: This is a real funds-loss defect affecting any user who overpays via claim() or batchClaim(). While the exact amount depends on user behavior, UI estimation errors, and gas price fluctuations that might lead to safety margins in msg.value, the pattern is a clear violation of the expected behavior that excess ETH should be refunded. The withdraw() function allows the protocol owner to extract these funds. Severity is HIGH because it results in permanent loss of user funds with no user-accessible recovery path.

vulnerabilities.title: Attacker Can Drain Trapped Factory ETH to Mint NFTs for Free
vulnerabilities.summary: The `claim` function in `PhiFactory` forwards the exact `mintFee` to an internal `this.merkleClaim{value: mintFee}` call. If a user overpays, the excess ETH is trapped in the factory because the inner refund logic only sees the exact `mintFee`. An attacker can exploit this trapped ETH by calling `claim` with `msg.value = 0`. The internal `this.merkleClaim{value: mintFee}` call will succeed by using the factory's trapped ETH balance, allowing the attacker to mint NFTs and generate protocol/creato

description.desc: External self-call `this.merkleClaim{value: mintFee}` inside a payable function without enforcing `msg.value == mintFee`

vulnerabilities.impact: HIGH severity. All ETH trapped in PhiFactory (from overpayments or direct transfers) can be drained by any user with valid claim credentials. The attacker receives free NFTs and can potentially extract reward payments by specifying attacker-controlled referral/verifier addresses. The owner's withdraw() function becomes a race condition against attackers. This is a direct loss of user funds that should have been refundable or withdrawable by the admin.

vulnerabilities.title: withdrawWithSig amount=0 Acts as Full Balance Withdrawal Sentinel
vulnerabilities.summary: In RewardControl._withdraw, amount==0 is treated as FULL_BALANCE sentinel, withdrawing the user's entire balance. When combined with withdrawWithSig, a signature authorizing amount=0 (which a user might interpret as 'no withdrawal') actually authorizes a full balance drain to the specified 'to' address.

description.desc: _withdraw: if(amount == FULL_BALANCE){amount = balance;} where FULL_BALANCE=0; withdrawWithSig signs amount=0

vulnerabilities.impact: Any user who signs a withdrawWithSig message with amount=0 unknowingly authorizes withdrawal of their entire PhiRewards balance. The severity depends on accumulated reward balances, which include artist rewards, referral rewards, verifier rewards, and curator rewards. The defect exists regardless of exploitation feasibility - the signed authorization does not faithfully represent the action performed.

vulnerabilities.title: Cred.updateCred Signature Does Not Include Chain ID Enabling Cross-Chain Replay
vulnerabilities.summary: The updateCred function validates a signature over signedData_ which encodes (expiresIn_, sender, _, credId, credURL) but does NOT include block.chainid. A valid signature used to update a cred on one chain can be replayed on another chain where the same Cred contract is deployed with matching credIds.

description.desc: signedData_ decoded as (expiresIn_, sender, _, credId, credURL) with no chainId; _recoverSigner uses raw hash without domain separator

vulnerabilities.impact: If exploitable, the attacker can modify credURL and royalty rates (up to 50% buy/sell royalty) on the target chain. This affects all future share trades on that cred, extracting up to 50% of trade value as creator royalty. The severity is MEDIUM because the preconditions (matching credId, same creator address) somewhat limit exploitability, but the code defect is real — the contract lacks any on-chain chain-specificity enforcement.

vulnerabilities.title: Unreachable Upgrade Path for Deployed NFT Clones
vulnerabilities.summary: The PhiNFT1155 contract is UUPS upgradeable and restricts upgrades to its owner (the Factory), but the Factory contract lacks any function to trigger an upgrade, rendering the NFTs permanently non-upgradeable.

description.desc: _authorizeUpgrade is onlyOwner (Factory), but Factory has no upgrade execution function

vulnerabilities.impact: MEDIUM severity. All deployed PhiNFT1155 clones are permanently non-upgradeable despite being designed with upgrade intent. If a vulnerability is discovered in PhiNFT1155 (mint logic, transfer logic, royalty logic), it cannot be patched. The protocol would need to deploy entirely new contracts and migrate all users and state. The UUPS-related storage and code in PhiNFT1155 is wasted gas during deployment without providing any benefit.

vulnerabilities.title: Cross-Chain Replay of Credential Creation Signatures
vulnerabilities.summary: The signature payload for creating a cred does not include the chain ID, allowing an attacker to intercept a valid creation signature on one chain and replay it to create the same cred on another chain.

description.desc: abi.decode(signedData_, ...) does not extract or validate block.chainid

vulnerabilities.impact: Unauthorized credential creation on chains where the protocol team did not intend the cred to exist. This bypasses the off-chain authorization process (the signer only signed for one specific chain). Created creds have bonding curves and can accumulate ETH through share trading, meaning unauthorized creds have real financial implications. The protocol's cred integrity across chains is compromised.

vulnerabilities.title: PhiNFT1155.createArtFromFactory can underflow/refund-revert if artCreateFee > msg.value, enabling DoS of art creation (factory passes msg.value blindly)
vulnerabilities.summary: PhiNFT1155.createArtFromFactory computes `artFee = phiFactoryContract.artCreateFee();` then unconditionally transfers `artFee` and later computes refund `(msg.value - artFee)` without checking `msg.value >= artFee`. If the factory call forwards insufficient ETH (or owner increases artCreateFee), refund subtraction underflows and reverts, or the transfer fails due to insufficient value, bricking createArt flows.

description.desc: No `require(msg.value >= artFee)`; computes `(msg.value - artFee)` for refund
Affected function: createArt(bytes,bytes,CreateConfig)

vulnerabilities.impact: No real impact. The code correctly handles all cases: sufficient ETH succeeds, insufficient ETH reverts. The max artCreateFee is 10,000 wei (~$0.00000003), making the scenario practically irrelevant. Missing explicit check is a code quality concern, not a functional defect.

vulnerabilities.title: Missing Reentrancy Guard on Individual Share Trades Enables Cross-Function Reentrancy
vulnerabilities.summary: Individual buy/sell functions (buyShareCred, sellShareCred) call _handleTrade which lacks a nonReentrant modifier (only batch operations have it). During a sell, after share balance and supply are decremented, ETH is sent to the curator via safeTransferETH. If the curator is a contract, its receive() can reenter buyShareCred for the same credId at the now-reduced supply, obtaining shares at a cheaper price. While the attacker must hold through the lock period, they acquire shares at an artificia

description.desc: _handleTrade: state updated (supply decremented), then curator_.safeTransferETH(price - protocolFee - creatorFee) — external call before protocolFee and creatorFee transfers. No nonReentrant modifier.

vulnerabilities.impact: LOW-MEDIUM practical impact despite the code defect being real. The missing nonReentrant on _handleTrade is an inconsistency with batch operations that constitutes a code defect. The reentrancy window exists and is exploitable to execute arbitrary cross-function calls. However, because state (share balances, supply) is updated before external calls, the most obvious attack (sell-then-buy) doesn't yield profit due to symmetric bonding curve pricing. The contract remains solvent after reentrancy. Future code modifications or more complex multi-step attacks could potentially exploit this window.

vulnerabilities.title: Any caller can drain CuratorRewardsDistributor 'royaltyfee' incentive by calling distribute() and receiving protocol cut + dust
vulnerabilities.summary: CuratorRewardsDistributor.distribute() computes royaltyfee from the per-credId balance and transfers (royaltyfee + rounding dust) to msg.sender, not to a designated protocol receiver. Because distribute() is permissionless, any address can repeatedly watch on-chain state and call distribute(credId) whenever balanceOf[credId] is non-zero to extract the royaltyfee. This is independent of mempool ordering (no public mempool needed) because balances are observable after deposits.

description.desc: _msgSender().safeTransferETH(royaltyfee + distributeAmount - actualDistributeAmount); // sends royaltyfee to caller

vulnerabilities.impact: No real impact as a vulnerability. The 1% caller incentive is a design choice, not a loss of funds. Curator rewards are correctly distributed (99% goes to curators). The protocol maintains governance control over the incentive rate. This is functionally equivalent to keeper incentives in protocols like Compound, Aave, or Maker.

vulnerabilities.title: BondingCurve curve formula can revert near asymptote, potentially freezing buys/sells at high supply and locking curator liquidity
vulnerabilities.summary: BondingCurve._curve uses division by (TOTAL_SUPPLY_FACTOR - targetAmount_). With targetAmount_ = (supply_ * 1 ether) and supply_ approaching TOTAL_SUPPLY_FACTOR/1 ether (1000), denominator approaches zero and will revert when equal. Cred caps MAX_SUPPLY=999, so (supply_ + amount_) * 1 ether can reach 1000 ether when supply_=999 and amount_=1, causing TOTAL_SUPPLY_FACTOR - targetAmount_ == 0 and a hard revert in getPrice/getPriceData for buys at the cap boundary. Depending on how supply and amoun

description.desc: (TOTAL_SUPPLY_FACTOR - targetAmount_) in denominator; targetAmount_ = (supply_ + amount_) * 1 ether

vulnerabilities.impact: LOW-MEDIUM severity in practice. The code defect is real (wrong ordering of checks, curve has exploitable asymptote), but practical impact is limited: (1) buys exceeding MAX_SUPPLY would be rejected anyway, (2) sells are unaffected, (3) the main impact is confusing error messages and broken view functions at the supply boundary. No funds are locked, no liquidity is frozen, but frontend/UI systems relying on price quote functions will fail ungracefully when supply=999.
